Project Name: Global Alliance for Vaccines and Immunisation (GAVI)
Description: Established in 2000, Gaviîalso known as the Vaccine Allianceîis a public_private global health partnership created to improve access to new and under_used vaccines for children in the worldís poorest countries. As part of its mission to save lives and protect peopleís health by increasing equitable and sustainable use of vaccines, Gavi has helped vaccinate more than 1.1_billion children across 78 lower_income countries, preventing more than 18.8_million future deaths. Gavi stands as the largest of its kind distributing donor funds toward vaccines and supports the immunization of nearly half of the worldís children, negotiating bulk pricing, reducing commercial risks for manufacturers, and strengthening health systems to achieve wider coverage. Its model has generated extraordinary returnsîeach dollar invested yielding significant health and economic valueîwhich has enabled graduating countries to become increasingly self_sufficient. Amid financial pressures on donors, Gavi continues to innovate, exploring mechanisms like vaccine bonds to sustain funding and ensure continued immunization progress.
Partners: World Health Organization (WHO), United Nations Childrenís Fund (UNICEF), World Bank, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, donor governments (e.g., USA, UK, Norway, Germany, Japan, France, EU), vaccine manufacturers, civil society organizations, and implementing country governments

Project Name: Global Mercury Partnership
Description: The Global Mercury Partnership, facilitated by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), is a global collaborative platform designed to reduce mercury use and emissions to protect human health and the environment. Established under the UNEPís mercury program, the partnership supports countries in implementing the Minamata Convention on Mercury by sharing knowledge, best practices, and technical assistance. It brings together governments, industry, academia, and civil society to foster coordinated action on mercury across sectors such as artisanal and small-scale gold mining, coal combustion, and industrial processes. The partnership focuses on capacity building, awareness raising, and promoting alternatives to mercury use, working to minimize risks and promote sound management of mercury-containing waste. By facilitating stakeholder engagement and fostering innovative solutions, the Global Mercury Partnership contributes to global efforts toward mercury pollution reduction, sustainable development, and public health protection.
Partners: UNEP, UNIDO, UNDP, World Health Organization (WHO), World Bank, International Labour Organization (ILO), governments of Norway, Switzerland, Japan, United States, and Canada, International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), Artisanal Gold Council, Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC), Zero Mercury Working Group (ZMWG), International POPs Elimination Network (IPEN), private sector and civil society partners engaged in mercury reduction initiatives

Project Name: Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria
Description: The Global Fund is a partnership organization designed to accelerate the end of AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria as epidemics. Founded in 2002, it mobilizes and invests more than US$4 billion annually to support programs run by local experts in more than 100 countries. The Global Fundís unique model brings together governments, civil society, the private sector, and people affected by these diseases to invest in sustainable health systems and drive impact where the need is greatest. It focuses on building resilient and sustainable systems for health, ensuring equitable access to prevention, treatment, and care, and advancing human rights and gender equality in health responses. By leveraging innovative financing and performance-based funding, the Global Fund enhances country ownership and accountability. Its work has contributed to significant reductions in disease burden and mortality, playing a pivotal role in global health progress and aligning with the Sustainable Development Goals.
Partners: World Health Organization (WHO), UNAIDS, UNICEF, World Bank, Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance, UNDP, governments of donor and implementing countries, private sector partners (including the Gates Foundation, Chevron, and Product (RED)), civil society and community-based organizations, and local health ministries across 100+ countries

Project Name: Stop TB Partnership
Description: The Stop TB Partnership is a United Nations_hosted entity, administered by UNOPS in Geneva, that brings together over 1,600 partner organizationsîincluding governments, research institutions, NGOs, civil society, community groups, and the private sectorîin a collective force to end tuberculosis globally. Founded in 2001, the Partnership provides unified leadership and representation for the TB community, advances global advocacy, and funds innovative strategies to find, treat, and cure those affected by TB. It shapes markets for diagnostics and medicines, enhancing access by reducing costs, improving forecasting, and preventing stock_outs. Through initiatives like the Global Drug Facility and TB_REACH, it expands access to quality tools and funds innovation tailored to reach underserved communities. The Partnership centers peopleîempowering communities and promoting rights_based, gender_transformative careîand continues to coordinate global efforts under a shared vision of a world free of TB by 2030.
Partners: UNOPS, World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, USAID, Gates Foundation, Global Drug Facility (GDF), TB REACH, KNCV Tuberculosis Foundation, Stop TB Partnership Board, governments of high-burden countries, research institutions, civil society organizations, and private-sector health innovators

Project Name: Roll-Back Malaria
Description: The RBM Partnership to End Malariaîoriginally launched as Roll Back Malaria in 1998 by WHO, UNICEF, UNDP, and the World Bankîis the global platform for coordinated action against malaria. Its mission is to mobilize partners, shape consensus, and scale up malaria control efforts at country levels to reduce morbidity and mortality through universal coverage and strengthened health systems. Over two decades, the Partnership spurred a more than 1,000% rise in malaria funding, distributed over one billion insecticide-treated bed nets across Africa, and saved seven million lives, preventing over a billion malaria cases. After surpassing its initial goal to Øroll backÆ malaria, RBM evolved to set a bolder ambition: ending malaria for good.
Partners: World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, UNDP, The World Bank, UNOPS, Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, USAID, Gates Foundation, African Leaders Malaria Alliance (ALMA), Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance, Malaria No More, PATH, Medicines for Malaria Venture (MMV), civil society coalitions, academic and research institutions, national malaria control programs, and private-sector partners

Project Name: Partnership for Maternal, Newborn & Child Healthò
Description: Established in 2005, PMNCH is the worldís largest alliance for womenís, childrenís, and adolescentsí health, encompassing over 1,250 partner institutions across ten constituencies, including governments, UN agencies, NGOs, youth groups, academic institutions, private sector entities, and funders. Hosted by WHO in Geneva, PMNCH provides a unique platform for stakeholders to align objectives, strategies, and resources to advance sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child, and adolescent healthîdriving effective financing, policies, and service delivery with no one left behind. Its 2021Å2025 strategy underscores the importance of global cooperation, human rights, and equity in rebuilding health systems amid challenges such as the COVID_19 pandemic.
Partners: Full list of partners dashboard: https://lookerstudio.google.com/u/0/reporting/67a60ad2-8106-449e-be59-305777a64644/page/sOs3C?s=iN75MZL0oy0

Project Name: Every Woman Every Child Initiative
Description: Every Woman Every Child is an unprecedented global movement, spearheaded by UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon, that mobilizes and intensifies international and national action by governments, the UN, multilaterals, the private sector, and civil society to address the major health challenges facing women, children, and adolescents. Its main objective is to end all preventable deaths of women, children, and adolescents within a generation and ensure that they thrive within a lifetime. The initiative has played a transformative roleîcredited with saving 2.4 million women and children from preventable deathsîthrough powerful advocacy, strategic partnerships, and an equity-driven focus on health indicators globally.
Partners: United Nations (UN Secretariat), World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, UNFPA, UN Women, UNAIDS, World Bank Group, Gavi (the Vaccine Alliance), Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, Partnership for Maternal, Newborn & Child Health (PMNCH), Every Woman Every Child Latin America and Caribbean, White Ribbon Alliance, Women Deliver, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Save the Children, International Planned Parenthood Federation (IPPF), Global Financing Facility (GFF), governments of UN Member States, youth networks, civil society organizations, and private-sector partners

Project Name: Scaling Up Nutrition
Description: Launched by the UN in 2010, the Scaling Up Nutrition (SUN) Movement is a country-led, multi-sectoral global push united behind the belief that good nutrition is the best investment of the future. It is a collaborative movement led by 60 countries and four Indian states, working across sectors including civil society, development partners, business enterprises, and researchers, to align national nutrition priorities and scale up policies and programs to end malnutrition by 2030 in an equitable and sustainable way.
Partners: 4,000 civil society organizations, 1,400 businesses, 5 UN agencies and a group of international donors and foundations

Project Name: Global Alliance for Improved Nutrition (GAIN)
Description: GAIN is a Swiss-based alliance launched at the United Nations in 2002, driven by a vision of a world without malnutrition. It works through publicÅprivate partnerships with governments, businesses, and civil society to transform food systems so they deliver more nutritious foods for all people, especially the most vulnerable. The allianceís mission is to improve the consumption of healthier and safe diets by enhancing the availability, affordability, desirability, and sustainability of nutritious foods and reducing consumption of unhealthy and unsafe options. Currently, GAIN-supported nutrition programs are reaching hundreds of millions of vulnerable people across more than 30 countries.
Partners: World Food Programme (WFP), UNICEF, World Health Organization (WHO), Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD), Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, USAID, UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), Government of the Netherlands, Government of Switzerland, Rockefeller Foundation, Scaling Up Nutrition (SUN) Movement, Global Financing Facility (GFF), private-sector partners (e.g., DSM, Cargill, Danone, Unilever), civil society organizations, and national ministries of health and agriculture in over 30 countries

Project Name: Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africaò(AGRA)
Description: AGRA is a proudly African-led, Africa-based partnershipîestablished in 2006îwith a mission to help millions of smallholder farmers and their families lift themselves out of poverty and hunger by delivering practical agricultural innovations. Operating under an African leadership model, the organization supports smallholder farmers by improving production, livelihoods, and food security through climate-smart solutions and access to markets. AGRA works closely with African governments and stakeholders to scale context-specific strategies across the continent.
Partners: African Union (AU), African Development Bank (AfDB), International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD), World Bank, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Rockefeller Foundation, USAID, UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), Mastercard Foundation, Syngenta Foundation for Sustainable Agriculture, Government of Kenya, Government of Ghana, Government of Nigeria, Government of Tanzania, African Fertilizer and Agribusiness Partnership (AFAP), CGIAR, International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) country programs, national farmer associations, and private agribusinesses across Africa

Project Name: Global Partnership for Education (GPE)
Description: The Global Partnership for Education is the worldís largest global fund and multi-stakeholder partnership solely dedicated to transforming education in lower-income countries, focused on delivering quality education so that every girl and boy can have hope, opportunity, and agency. It supports governments to strengthen education systems by promoting inclusive policy dialogue, aligning external financing with national priorities, and providing both funding and technical expertise to catalyze reforms and lock in long-term change. GPE helps countries develop sector-wide education plans, linking donor support to policy performance and accountability. In responding to crisesîlike the COVID_19 pandemicîit works to ensure that vulnerable students, especially the poorest girls, remain in learning environments, supports safe school reopening, and helps governments safeguard education spending.
Partners: World Bank (host organization), UNESCO, UNICEF, UNHCR, Global Education Cluster, Education Cannot Wait (ECW), Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie (OIF), African Union, European Union, OECD, Global Campaign for Education (GCE), Education International, Save the Children, Plan International, World Vision, USAID, UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), Global Affairs Canada, NORAD, Australian DFAT, Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA), Franceís AFD, Germanyís BMZ, Dubai Cares, Open Society Foundations, LEGO Foundation, Hewlett Foundation, CIFF, and multiple national education ministries in over 90 partner countries

Project Name: World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA)
Description: Established in 1999 and co-founded by the International Olympic Committee and governments from over 140 nations, WADA leads a collaborative worldwide movement for doping-free sport. Its mission is to develop, harmonize, coordinate, and monitor anti-doping rules and policies across all sports and countries, driven by a unique partnership between the Sport Movement and public authorities. WADAís work includes scientific research, education, capacity development, oversight of the World Anti-Doping Code, and tools such as the Anti-Doping Education & Learning Platform (ADEL) and the Anti-Doping Administration & Management System (ADAMS).
Partners: International Olympic Committee (IOC), UNESCO, United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC), International Testing Agency (ITA), Interpol, World Health Organization (WHO), Council of Europe, International Paralympic Committee (IPC), national anti-doping organizations (NADOs), regional anti-doping organizations (RADOs), international sports federations, national governments of over 140 countries, and major sporting bodies (FIFA, World Athletics, FINA, UCI, etc.)

Project Name: The Global Handwashing Partnership (GHP)
Description: The Global Handwashing Partnership is a coalition of international stakeholders dedicated to promoting handwashing with soap and recognizing hygiene as a fundamental pillar of international development and public health. Established in 2001, the partnership brings together organizations from multiple sectors, including government agencies, multilateral organizations, corporations, non-governmental organizations, and academic institutions. The GHP works to save childrenís lives and improve health by promoting the important habit of handwashing with soap. Its mission is to ensure that handwashing with soap becomes a routine part of everyday life, especially in communities where it can have the most impact. Through advocacy, behavior change campaigns, and the promotion of Global Handwashing Day, the GHP aims to increase awareness and encourage the practice of handwashing with soap to prevent infections and save lives. By collaborating with various stakeholders, the GHP strives to integrate hand hygiene into national and global health agendas, ensuring that it receives the attention and resources it deserves. Their efforts contribute to achieving broader health and development goals, including reducing child mortality and improving overall public health outcomes.
Partners: FHI 360, UNICEF, USAID, World Bank, WHO, CDC, WaterAid, Unilever, Procter & Gamble, Save the Children, WSSCC, Lifebuoy, academic institutions, and national health ministries

Project Name: Harnessing Non-State Actors for Better Health for the Poor platform (HANSHEP)
Description: HANSHEP is a collaborative initiative that brings together development agencies, country governments, and non-state actors to improve health outcomes for the poor by leveraging the contributions of non-state actors in delivering better healthcare. The platform focuses on enhancing the performance of non-state actors in the health sector through evidence generation, policy influence, and knowledge sharing. HANSHEP aims to create a more effective and equitable health system by recognizing and strengthening the role of non-state actors, including non-governmental organizations, private sector entities, and community-based organizations. Through its activities, HANSHEP seeks to inform policies, disseminate evidence, and facilitate learning and collaboration among stakeholders. By fostering partnerships and promoting the integration of non-state actors into health systems, HANSHEP contributes to the achievement of universal health coverage and improved health outcomes for underserved populations.
Partners: UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), World Bank, USAID, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Rockefeller Foundation, GIZ, African Health Markets for Equity (AHME), and national health ministries

Project Name: Global Financing Facility
Description: The Global Financing Facility is a country-led partnership hosted at the World Bank that fights poverty and inequity by advancing the health and rights of women, children, and adolescents. The GFF supports countries to strengthen health systems and improve access to care through prioritized plans, aligned public and private financing, and policy reform. It provides technical assistance and funding to partner countries through grants from its dedicated multidonor trust fund, ensuring that resources are directed towards the most pressing health needs and enabling countries to implement comprehensive health plans that address reproductive, maternal, newborn, child, and adolescent health and nutrition. By focusing on country-led solutions and fostering collaboration among governments, development partners, and the private sector, the GFF aims to accelerate progress towards universal health coverage and the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals.
Partners: World Bank, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, Gavi, the Global Fund, USAID, Gates Foundation, UPS, Rockefeller Foundation, Canada, Norway, Germany, Japan, the Netherlands

Project Name: Pineapple Homes Consortium
Description: The Pineapple Homes Consortium focuses on retrofitting homes in the UK to improve energy efficiency and sustainability, particularly in the social housing sector. Pineapple collaborates with various partner organizations in the private sector such as Places for People, NatWest and Centrica. The partnership unites housing, finance, and technology industries to address costs, skills shortages, and data gaps. By leveraging subsidies and economies of scale, it has developed an affordable, scalable model for net-zero retrofits, creating healthier living environments and reducing health risks. Geographical Scope: National, United Kingdom
Partners: Places for People, NatWest, British Gas, Sero, Tallarna, Pineapple Sustainable Partnerships

Project Name: Sanitation and Water for All (SWA)
Description: Sanitation and Water for All is a global, multi-stakeholder partnership of governments, civil society, private sector, UN agencies, external support agencies and research and learning institutions, united around a single vision: sanitation, water and hygiene for all, always, everywhere. The Partnership works to build and sustain political will to eliminate inequalities in WASH (water, sanitation and hygiene), champion multi-stakeholder approaches toward universal access, and rally stakeholders to strengthen system performance and attract new investments. SWA brings together diverse constituenciesîfrom national governments, utilities/regulators, donors, development banks, private sector and civil societyîto support government-led sector planning, coordinate action, enhance transparency and accountability through a unique Mutual Accountability Mechanism, and ensure that investments in water and sanitation are used effectively. Through high-level meetings and country-level processes, SWA advocates for prioritization of water and sanitation in national development agendas, supports capacity development and institutional strengthening, and fosters inclusive governanceîespecially ensuring that vulnerable populations, including women and girls, are given voice. In doing so, SWA seeks to accelerate progress toward the water and sanitation targets of the 2030 Agenda.
Partners: Governments, utilities & regulators, external support agencies/donors, civil society organisations, research & learning institutions, private sector

Project Name: Integrated Monitoring Initiative for SDG 6
Description: The Integrated Monitoring Initiative for SDG 6 is a concerted effort coordinated by UN-Water that brings together the United Nations agencies mandated to compile country data on water and sanitation, aiming to accelerate the achievement of the global goal of ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all. The initiative supports countries in monitoring water- and sanitation-related issues by providing guidance, technical capacity, and methodological support for collecting, analyzing and reporting the full range of SDG 6 global indicatorsîfrom safely managed drinking water and sanitation, to wastewater treatment, water-use efficiency, ecosystem health, transboundary water cooperation, and stakeholder participation. IMI-SDG6 emphasizes evidence-based policy making: by increasing the availability of high-quality, disaggregated and comparable data, it strengthens accountability, informs investments and decision-making, and helps governments and stakeholders to track progress, identify gaps, and mobilize resources. Through coordinated data gathering, capacity development, and support to national monitoring frameworks, IMI-SDG6 plays a critical role in transforming raw data into actionable insightsîensuring that water, sanitation and ecosystem-management policies reflect real-world conditions, and that progress towards SDG 6 remains transparent, measurable and inclusive across countries.
Partners: Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE), UNESCO, UN-Habitat, UNICEF, World Health Organization (WHO), World Meteorological Organization (WMO)

Project Name: World Water Quality Alliance
Description: The World Water Quality Alliance is a global, voluntary, multi-stakeholder network convened by the UN Environment Programme (UNEP), bringing together science, policy, civil society, private sector and other stakeholders to strengthen evidence-based action on water quality in support of the 2030 Agenda. The Alliance works to bridge scientific knowledge and data with concrete management and policy actions, promoting open data access, collaborative research, and development of tools, guidelines and capacity-building resources that support monitoring, assessment, protection and restoration of freshwater quality. Through its various workstreams, WWQA supports countries and communities to identify water-quality risks, develop tailored water-quality management and remediation approaches, and safeguard both human health and ecosystem integrity. The Alliance emphasizes inclusive participation, involving local communities, youth and diverse stakeholders to ensure water-quality solutions are grounded in local contexts and driven by democratic processes. By linking global expertise with local actions and prioritizing data-driven water-quality interventions, WWQA contributes to sustainable water management, pollution mitigation, ecosystem protection and the broader goal of safe, clean water for all.
Partners: United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), World Meteorological Organization (WMO), United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), European Commission Joint Research Centre (JRC), research institutions, civil society organizations, private sector entities, 50+ other organisations

Project Name: Water and Climate Coalition
Description: The Water and Climate Coalition is a multi-stakeholder initiative established to address interlinked water and climate challenges by promoting integrated waterÅclimate action, building data and information systems, strengthening operational capacity, and informing policy development. The Coalition brings together UN bodies, intergovernmental organizations, NGOs, civil society and private-sector actors to collaboratively implement solutions across data & information, capacity development, financing, governance and innovation streams. Its work aims to strengthen resilience of water systems in the face of climate change, support sustainable water management, and align water-sector planning with climate adaptation and mitigation efforts. By offering a flexible, action-oriented platform rather than a bureaucratic structure, the Water and Climate Coalition fosters pragmatic, cross-sectoral collaboration to support SDG 6 (water and sanitation for all) and climate-related environmental sustainability goalsîensuring water security in a changing climate and promoting long-term resilience through integrated approaches.
Partners: World Meteorological Organization (WMO), World Health Organisation (WHO), United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE), Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), United Nations University (UNU), United Nations Childrenís Emergency Fund (UNICEF), International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD), Global Water Partnership

Project Name: Hand Hygiene for All initiative
Description: Hand Hygiene for All is a global initiative led by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the UNICEF, established to implement WHOís global hand hygiene recommendations, promote lasting infrastructure and behaviour change, and make hand hygiene an integral part of national health, sanitation and development planning beyond the immediate pandemic response. The initiative emphasises a comprehensive three-stage approachîResponding to urgent sanitary needs, Rebuilding infrastructure and services, and Reimagining hand hygiene in societies long-term. Through securing political leadership, strengthening institutional and regulatory environments, ensuring access to hygiene infrastructure (soap, water, handwashing stations) across homes, health care facilities, schools, workplaces and public spaces, and applying evidence-based behaviour-change strategies, Hand Hygiene for All strives to prevent disease transmission (including COVID-19 and antimicrobial resistance), improve public health, and embed hygiene as a foundational public good. The initiative aligns closely with broader Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) efforts and supports achievement of global health equity, universal hygiene access and sustainable development.
Partners: World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, Africa Civil Society Network on Water and Sanitation, AMCOW, COVID-19 Hygiene Hub, Global Handwashing Partnership, International Labour Organization (ILO), Sanitation and Water for All (SWA), UNHCR, WaterAid, World Bank, World Economic Forum

Project Name: Diabetes Fiji Inc.
Description: Diabetes Fiji Inc. is a Fijian charitable trust, re-established in 2012 to continue the work of the former National Diabetes Foundation. As a member of the International Diabetes Federation (IDF), DFIís mission is to steer Fijiís national solidarity in combating diabetes by promoting prevention, care and management. It empowers people living with or at risk of diabetes through awareness campaigns, community outreach, education on risk factors, and advocacy for healthy lifestyles. DFI supports health-care systems to provide affordable and sustainable services, and runs projects such as foot-care clinics, telemedicine support, and diabetic-care screening. It also emphasises public-policy advocacy to create environments conducive to diabetes control and works to engage caregivers, families, and communities in supporting people with diabetes.
Partners: Ministry of Health and Medical Services (Fiji), Secretariat of the Pacific Community, USAID, JICA, LDS Charities, International Republican Institute, Insulin for Life Australia, World Diabetes Foundation, Aspen Medical, Kontiki Finance, Makan Drugs Ltd, Equal Meds, community peer-support groups, local pharmacies and medical suppliers

Project Name: SDG Partnership Platform (Kenya)
Description: The SDG Partnership Platform (Kenya) was launched in 2017 by the Government of Kenya with support from the UN system as a flagship mechanism to accelerate achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals through large-scale, cross-sectoral collaboration. The Platform convenes government agencies, private sector actors, philanthropy, civil society, academia, development partners and UN entities to co-create ØSDG Accelerator WindowsÆ focused initially on Primary Healthcare (universal health coverage) and Food & Nutrition Security, and later planned to extend into affordable housing, manufacturing and skills training. Its mission is to mobilize innovative financing, partnerships and impact investment to deliver social services and development outcomes under Kenyaís ØBig FourÆ national agenda î shifting SDG efforts from traditional funding to sustainable financing and partnership-driven implementation.
Partners: Government of Kenya, United Nations Country Team in Kenya, UN DP, UNFPA, UNICEF, WHO, development partners, private sector companies (e.g., Philips, Merck, AstraZeneca, Huawei), civil society organisations, philanthropy (e.g., Rockefeller Foundation)

Project Name: NCD Alliance
Description: The NCD Alliance is a global civil-society network, formally established in 2009 in Geneva, that unites hundreds of organisations worldwide across disease groups, risk factors, regions and sectors to tackle noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) î such as cancer, diabetes, chronic respiratory disease, cardiovascular disease, mental health and more. Its mission is to drive global and national action on NCD prevention and care so that everyone can live a healthy and productive life, free from preventable suffering, stigma, disability and death caused by NCDs. NCDA uses targeted advocacy, global convenings, and partnership-building to push for stronger health-promoting policies, increased and equitable access to prevention and care, sustainable financing, and civil-society engagement, while working with governments, UN agencies, NGOs, the private sector and people living with NCDs. Through global advocacy, capacity building, evidence generation and multisectoral collaboration, NCDA seeks to reduce premature mortality, promote health equity and ensure that NCDs remain a priority under the 2030 Agenda and universal health-coverage frameworks.
Partners: The Union Against Tuberculosis and Lung Disease, International Diabetes Federation (IDF), Union for International Cancer Control (UICC), World Heart Federation, American College of Cardiology, American Heart Association, Cancer Research UK, FDI World Dental Federation, Global Alliance for Patient Access (GAfPA), Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD), International Federation of Psoriasis Associations (IFPA), Resolve to Save Lives (RTSL), The George Institute for Global Health, Vital Strategies, World Obesity Federation, Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (SIDA), AstraZeneca, Boehringer Ingelheim, Bristol-Myers Squibb, Eli Lilly and Company, Novo Nordisk, Roche, Sanofi, Vantive, Viatris, Beyond Type 1, Bloomberg Philanthropies, Clean Air Fund, Novo Nordisk Foundation, Helmsley Charitable Trust, World Diabetes Foundation

Project Name: The Fleming Fund
Description: The Fleming Fund is a UK aid programme hosted by the UK Department of Health and Social Care that supports low- and middle-income countries to generate, share and use high-quality antimicrobial resistance (AMR) data; working through country grants, laboratory and surveillance strengthening, capacity building, regional reference centres and technical assistance, the Fund invests in national AMR surveillance systems, quality assurance, data analysis and evidence translation so policymakers, clinicians and regulators can design effective stewardship, infection prevention and control, and public-health responses that reduce the threat of drug resistance.
Partners: UK Department of Health & Social Care (DHSC), Mott MacDonald (Fund Manager), United Nations agencies (FAO, WHO, WOAH), national governments, public health institutes, reference laboratories, universities, civil society organisations, private laboratories, AMR experts

Project Name: Aliarse (Costa Rica)
Description: Fundacin Aliarse is a Costa Rican multi-stakeholder foundation that mobilises the private sector toward climate action and sustainable development by promoting publicÅprivate collaboration, corporate social responsibility and capacity building; its mission is to improve peopleís well-being through multisector alliances, delivering programs on waste management, water stewardship, social inclusion and training courses, and partnering with businesses, public institutions and civil society to design locally relevant projects that strengthen resilience, close equity gaps and foster sustainability at community and national levels.
Partners: Costa Rican Ministry of Education, Costa Rican Ministry of Health, private companies in Costa Rica, national NGOs, universities, international donors

Project Name: Grow Africa
Description: Grow Africa is a publicÅprivate partnership platform that fosters inclusive and sustainable agricultural development across Africa by connecting stakeholders (governments, private sector, farmers, civil society) around investment, agribusiness growth and smallholder support. Its approach encourages responsible agribusiness investment, value-chain development, technical assistance and inclusive growth to raise productivity, incomes and food security, with the aim of transforming agriculture into a driver of sustainable development and shared prosperity across rural communities.
Partners: Governments of multiple African countries, African Union, African Development Bank, World Economic Forum, AGRA (Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa), Private agribusiness companies (e.g., Yara International, Syngenta, Dangote Group), Civil-society organizations, Farmer organizations, Impact investors, Donor agencies.

Project Name: Grow Asia
Description: Grow Asia is a multi-stakeholder partnership platform established by the World Economic Forum and regional partners to catalyze inclusive, resilient and sustainable food systems across Southeast Asia. By brokering collaborations among public and private actors, farmers, civil society, academia and investors, Grow Asia supports thematic areas such as agri-food innovation, womenís economic empowerment, responsible investing and climate-resilient agriculture. Its network î with more than 700 public, private and civil-society organisations î facilitates knowledge sharing, policy engagement, sustainable agribusiness, value-chain transformation and investment to strengthen smallholder livelihoods and build regional food security.
Partners: ASEAN Secretariat, World Economic Forum, National governments in Southeast Asia, International NGOs (Mercy Corps, Pact, etc.), Agribusiness companies (Unilever, Syngenta, Cargill, Olam), Local SMEs, Farmer organizations, Development agencies (USAID, DFAT, GIZ), Research institutions

Project Name: Scaling Up Nutrition (SUN) Movement
Description: The Scaling Up Nutrition Movement is a global, multi-stakeholder effort to eliminate all forms of malnutrition by aligning governments, civil society, donors, business and researchers behind common national nutrition strategies. By promoting investments, evidence-based policies and coordinated action î especially targeting maternal and child nutrition, food systems, and under-nourished populations î SUN seeks to improve access to adequate, nutritious diets, reduce stunting and wasting, and ensure sustainable nutrition outcomes as part of long-term development and health equity.
Partners: 65+ National Governments, United Nations agencies (UNICEF, WHO, WFP, FAO, UNDP), Donor Network (USAID, DFID/FCDO, EU, Gates Foundation), Civil Society Network (Save the Children, Concern Worldwide, CARE), SUN Business Network (over 1,500 companies), Academia and research institutions

Project Name: The UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network
Description: SDSN mobilizes the global knowledge community î universities, research institutes and experts î to drive practical, science-based solutions for sustainable development under the auspices of the UN Secretary-General. Founded in 2012, SDSN works at the intersection of science, policy and development practice to promote the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the Paris Agreement, offering education and research, policy analysis, data and monitoring, and global cooperation. Through more than 2,000 member institutions across 59 national and regional networks, SDSN translates academic expertise into real-world sustainable-development pathways, supports governments and multilateral institutions in formulating SDG-compliant strategies, fosters peer-to-peer learning, and acts as a hub for global solution initiatives in areas such as climate and energy; food and land use; health; sustainable cities; governance; and sustainable finance.
Partners: UN agencies, multilateral financing institutions, governments, academia, civil society, and the private sector. Some funders include: Bezos Earth Fund, Bundesministerium fr Wirtschaft und Klimaschutz (BMWK) and Internationale Klimaschutzinitiative (IKI), Charles Stewart Mott Foundation, Cleveland Foundation, CONCITO, Corporacin Andina de Fomento (CAF), Education Above All Foundation (EAA), Ernesto Illy Foundation, Esri (Environmental Systems Research Institute, Inc.), European Climate, Infrastructure and Environment Executive Agency (CARMINE), European Commission - European Climate, Infrastructure and Environment Executive Agency (ARSINOE, IMPETUS & CHOICE), European Commission through IDDRI (funded by the European Union), European Education and Culture Executive Agency (EACEA) (CATALYST & BuildSkills), European Network of Political Foundations (ENoP), Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office of the UK Government (FCDO), Global Energy Interconnection Development & Cooperation Organization (GEIDCO), Global Environment Facilityís Food, Land Use, and Restoration Impact Program, Global Water Partnership Organisation, Gordon & Betty Moore Foundation, Harold Mitchell Foundation, Hearst Foundations (supporting U.S. programs), Jeffrey Cheah Foundation, Monash University, Prime Ministerís Office of the United Arab Emirates, Republique du Benin, Robert Bosch Stiftung GmbH, Siemens Gamesa Renewable Energy, The Food & Agriculture Organization (FAO), The Gates Foundation, The Prime Ministerís Office of the United Arab Emirates, The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), The William & Flora Hewlett Foundation, The William, Jeff, and Jennifer Gross Family Foundation, The World Bank, Trustees of Columbia University in the City of New York, United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), University of Tokyo (through the Institute for Future Initiative), among others.

Project Name: International Federation on Ageing (IFA)
Description: IFA, founded in 1973, is a global non-governmental organisation focused on ageing-related issues worldwide, mobilizing expertise, data and advocacy to influence policies that improve the quality of life of older people everywhere. With consultative status at the UN, IFA brings together NGOs, governments, academia and private sector actors to promote the rights, choices and well-being of older persons, and to embed ageing issues in global development and ageing-policy frameworks.
Partners: Full list of partners: https://ifa.ngo/membership/partners/network-partners/

Project Name: Global Partnership for Sustainable Development Data
Description: GPSDD is a global initiative that brings together governments, civil society, the private sector and data experts to improve the availability, quality and use of data for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). By supporting data generation, open data access, capacity-building, data governance and analytics, the Partnership enables better monitoring, planning and accountability for SDG implementation; it helps countries and stakeholders make evidence-based decisions, track progress, highlight inequalities, and direct resources where they are most needed î strengthening the data backbone for sustainable development globally.
Partners: LinkedIn, IDA, Save the Children, Government of Nigeria

Project Name: Center for Global Health & Development
Description: The Center for Global Health & Development focuses on advancing global health through research, policy advocacy, and capacity strengthening, aiming to improve health outcomes and strengthen health systems in low- and middle-income countries. Through analysis, data-driven research, and partnerships, it addresses global development and health challenges î including disease prevention, health equity and systemic improvements î contributing to broader sustainable development by promoting effective, evidence-based health interventions and global health governance.
Partners: Private sector companies, governments, development financing institutions, private capital investors, development banks, donors and foundations, NGOs, UN agencies and multi-lateral groups.

Project Name: Center for Global Development
Description: The Center for Global Development is a non-partisan, independent think tank that conducts rigorous economic research to reduce global poverty and inequality. It works to influence the policies and practices of governments, international institutions, and corporations by producing actionable research and policy proposals on issues including global health, aid effectiveness, trade, migration, climate and development finance. CGD aims to make globalization work for the poor by combining data-driven analysis, thought leadership and engagement with decision-makers to ensure that development assistance, global economic governance and development policies deliver tangible improvements for vulnerable populations worldwide.
Partners: Australian Government, EU, Gates Foundation, Bloomberg Philanthropies, Ford Foundation, French Development Agency (AFD), Canadian Government, Hilton Foundation, IKEA, Inter-American Development Bank, Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation, Open Philanthropy, among others.

Project Name: Sustainable Sanitation and Water Alliance (SuSanA)
Source: High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development
Description: SuSanA is a global multi-stakeholder network that promotes sustainable sanitation and water solutions through knowledge exchange, capacity building, and collaborative policy dialogue. Bringing together governments, NGOs, researchers, and practitioners, the alliance focuses on environmentally sound sanitation systems, inclusive service delivery, and innovations that support health and climate resilience. It serves as an open platform for sharing best practices, coordinating sector approaches, and strengthening the integration of sanitation within broader development priorities, especially in low- and middle-income countries.
Partners: Full list of partners: https://www.susana.org/community/partners

Project Name: 2030 Water Resources Group (2030WRG)
Source: High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development
Description: The 2030 WRG is a global publicÅprivate partnership that supports countries in addressing water scarcity through collaborative water governance, innovative financing, and sector-scale reform. By convening government agencies, companies, and civil society actors, it facilitates data-driven dialogue and helps design practical solutions for sustainable water use across agriculture, industry, and urban systems. The initiative aims to close water demandÅsupply gaps and build resilient water management frameworks aligned with long-term development goals.
Partners: World Economic Forum, International Finance Corporation (World Bank Group), Nestl, PepsiCo, The Coca-Cola Company, McKinsey & Company, Barilla Group, New Holland Agriculture, SABMiller, Standard Chartered, Syngenta, Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation, other bilateral and multilateral donors, participating national governments

Project Name: New Vision for Agriculture
Source: High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development
Description: The New Vision for Agriculture, initiated through the World Economic Forum, promotes a model of agricultural development that boosts productivity, improves environmental sustainability, and supports inclusive growth for farming communities. It brings together governments, companies, and farmer organizations to co-create value-chain partnerships that tackle food security, climate challenges, and rural livelihoods. The initiative emphasizes innovation, market access, and cross-sector platforms that help modernize agriculture while protecting natural resources.
Partners: World Economic Forum, roughly 30Å31 partner companies from the food and agriculture value chain including Nestl, PepsiCo, Unilever, Cargill, Archer Daniels Midland, Walmart, Yara International, Monsanto/Bayer, Syngenta, Olam, national governments engaged through country-level partnerships, farmer organisations and other local stakeholders

Project Name: INASP Å Research and knowledge at the heart of development
Source: High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development
Description: INASP is an international organization promoting research equity and strengthening knowledge systems in low- and middle-income countries. It collaborates with universities, research institutions, and governments to improve research capacity, access to information, and the use of evidence in policymaking. INASP supports more inclusive and locally led knowledge ecosystems by providing training, digital tools, and partnerships that empower researchers and decision-makers to address development challenges.
Partners: Government ministries, universities, research institutions, NGOs, think-tanks, national organisations and private-sector actors across Africa, Asia and Latin America; partners listed include organisations such as 3ie Å International Initiative for Impact Evaluation and many others

Project Name: Alliance for Responsible Science (OEAD) Austria
Description: On 17 June 2015, the Federal Ministry of Education, Science and Research founded the Alliance for Responsible Science. The aim of this alliance is to foster a change of the Austrian research system towards Responsible Research and Innovation. The OeAD is responsible for the coordination of the alliance.
